evidence: Large runtime seam bundles are embedded and forwarded across modules (`importerRuntime` embeds orchestration ops in `goseg/importer/importer.go`, `workflowRuntime` embeds orchestration ops in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, and those bundles are defined centrally in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`).

Global-singleton and injected-runtime models coexist in the same subsystems (`DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime` in `goseg/broadcast/state.go`, `defaultEventRuntime` in `goseg/docker/events/events.go`, process-wide runtime context in `goseg/config/config.go`), which weakens abstraction boundaries and makes ownership of state ambiguous.

Multiple wrapper layers provide mainly pass-through behavior with repeated nil/resolve guards (for example transition/broadcast wrappers in `goseg/broadcast/transitions.go` and runtime resolver wrappers in `goseg/main.go` and `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go`).

Critical loops are branch-heavy and mix collection, policy, and recovery in one function (`check502Loop` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_health.go`, `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime` in `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`), reducing directness and making edge behavior harder to reason about.

Long-lived routines capture configuration once and then run indefinitely with stale policy values (`CheckVersionLoopWithContext` in `goseg/routines/version.go` computes interval/channel once; backup loops in `goseg/routines/system/system.go` similarly compute interval once).

Per-call runtime preparation/validation adds repeated control-flow ceremony around straightforward operations (`prepareForUse` in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go`, repeated resolve/validate in workflow runtime paths in `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`).

Lifecycle starter APIs use inconsistent contracts for similar responsibilities: `StartBroadcastLoop() bool` in `goseg/broadcast/loop.go:107`, `StartDockerHealthLoops(ctx) error` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go:50`, `StartBackupRoutines()` (no return) and `StartBackupRoutinesWithContext(ctx) error` in `goseg/routines/system/system.go:17-31`.

Some `Start*WithContext` functions block until `ctx.Done()` and always return nil (`StartBackupRoutinesWithContext`, `StartChopRoutinesWithContext`), while others return operational errors from workers (`StartVersionSubsystemWithContext`, `StartDockerSubsystem`), making failure semantics unpredictable at call sites such as startup orchestration (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:520-551`).

Broadcast transition entry points use variadic optional runtime parameters (`ApplyBroadcastTransition(...runtime ...*BroadcastStateRuntime)`) while returning hard errors when omitted (`goseg/broadcast/transitions.go:19-29`), which is a confusing API shape.

Acronym casing conventions drift across sibling modules: WiFi/Wifi coexist (`NewWiFiRuntimeService`, `IsWiFiEnabled`, `HasWifiDevice`) in `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` and `goseg/main.go:238-239`.

Wireguard naming also mixes acronym styles (`WG`, `Wg`, `Wireguard`) in public seams and config fields: `CreateDefaultWGConfFn`, `WriteWgConf`, `GetWgConfFn`, `WgOn`, `WgRegistered` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go`, and `goseg/config/config.go`.

Log-stream naming mixes `SysLog`, `SystemLog`, and docker-log names (`SysLogStreamerWithRuntime`, `SystemLogMessages`) across `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go` and startup runtime wiring in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:525-550`.

`ConstructWiFiInfo(dev string) error` mutates runtime state and does not construct/return a value (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go:86-102`), while neighboring APIs (`ListWiFiSSIDs`, `IsWiFiEnabled`) are query-shaped; this name misleads callers.

`Start*WithContext` naming suggests consistent behavior, but implementations vary between fire-and-forget wrappers and worker-supervision loops (`goseg/routines/system/system.go`, `goseg/routines/version.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go`), so names under-communicate behavior.

Abbreviated public names like `WgOn`, `GetWgConfFn`, `WriteWgConf` reduce intent clarity compared to fully expanded domain terms used elsewhere (`WireguardRuntime`, `LoadWireguard`) in `goseg/config/config.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, and `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go`.

findings.summary: Cross-module runtime bundles are too broad, creating pass-through abstractions that couple unrelated domains.
evidence: `RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeSnapshotOps`, and related bundles in orchestration are large aggregates and then embedded wholesale into importer/workflow runtimes rather than exposing narrow use-case interfaces.
`defaultImporterRuntime` and `newWorkflowRuntime` mostly wire orchestration callbacks directly, so call depth increases without adding policy or translation.
Startup wiring further fans this pattern out by composing many callback groups, increasing abstraction surface area and dependency reach.
findings.suggestion: Split the large runtime bundles into small role-focused interfaces per use case (for example, import-provision ops, transition-publish ops, ship-status ops) and inject only those into each module; keep orchestration package internals behind adapter constructors.

findings.summary: Singleton state and injected runtime abstractions are mixed in the same flows, making boundaries and lifecycle ownership inconsistent.
evidence: Broadcast exposes a global default runtime (`DefaultBroadcastStateRuntime`) while also supporting injected runtimes.
Events exposes a process-global broker via `sync.OnceValue` (`DefaultEventRuntime`) while also allowing delegated brokers.
Config runtime context is process-global mutable state, while bootstrap code in `main.go` builds additional runtime structs and snapshots around it.
findings.suggestion: Choose one primary ownership model per subsystem (explicit injected runtime or singleton service locator) and push the other behind compatibility adapters; make lifecycle/startup ordering explicit in one composition root.

findings.summary: Operational loops combine polling, state mutation, policy checks, and recovery in single large control-flow blocks.
evidence: `check502Loop` nests readiness checks, config reads, network calls, strike counting, and recovery side effects in one loop body.
`runBroadcastTickWithRuntime` performs session gating, multi-source collection, transition preservation, mutation, and broadcast dispatch in one function.
`CheckVersionLoopWithContext` interleaves periodic scheduling with update policy and failure-threshold logic, reducing locality of intent.
findings.suggestion: Refactor each loop into explicit staged steps (`collect`, `decide`, `act`) with small pure helpers for policy decisions and separate effectful executors; keep loop bodies orchestration-only.

findings.summary: Frequently called paths repeatedly resolve and validate runtime dependencies, adding control-flow noise and hidden overhead.
evidence: WiFi service methods all route through `prepareForUse`, which resolves runtime and validates callback completeness on every call.
Workflow helpers repeatedly call `resolveWorkflowRuntime` inside nested helper functions, even when runtime was already prepared.
Broadcast transition/mutation wrappers repeatedly perform variadic runtime resolution and nil checks before straightforward state mutation.
findings.suggestion: Validate runtimes once at construction/startup and pass validated structs through hot paths; keep per-call logic focused on domain work, not dependency re-resolution.

findings.summary: Startup/lifecycle APIs expose inconsistent return and blocking contracts for similar operations.
evidence: `StartBroadcastLoop()` returns `bool` (`goseg/broadcast/loop.go:107-133`), `StartDockerHealthLoops(ctx)` returns `error` (`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go:50-83`), and `StartBackupRoutines()` returns nothing (`goseg/routines/system/system.go:17-20`).

`StartBackupRoutinesWithContext` and `StartChopRoutinesWithContext` block on `<-ctx.Done()` and return nil (`goseg/routines/system/system.go:22-31,68-75`), while `StartVersionSubsystemWithContext` and `StartDockerSubsystem` return worker errors (`goseg/routines/version.go:35-65`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go:85-129`).
Startup orchestration wires these mixed contracts into one background-service surface (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go:520-551`), making caller expectations inconsistent.
findings.suggestion: Define one lifecycle contract for starter APIs (for example: `StartX(ctx) error` where return means initialization/supervision failure, and cancellation is signaled only by context). Apply it across broadcast, routines, and docker subsystem, and adapt startup orchestrator wiring to the unified contract.

findings.summary: Acronym casing conventions are inconsistent across public symbols (WiFi/Wifi, WG/Wg, SysLog/SystemLog).
evidence: WiFi symbols use both `WiFi` and `Wifi` variants: `NewWiFiRuntimeService`, `IsWiFiEnabled` vs `HasWifiDevice` (`goseg/system/*`, `goseg/main.go:238-239`).

Wireguard symbols mix `WG` and `Wg` forms in adjacent APIs: `CreateDefaultWGConfFn`, `GetWgConfFn`, `WriteWgConf`, `WgOn` (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go`, `goseg/config/config.go`).
Logstream uses `SysLog*` naming while runtime fields expose `SystemLogMessages` (`goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go:41,133,190`).
findings.suggestion: Adopt a single acronym style guide for exported identifiers in these modules, then perform a coordinated rename pass (including runtime seam fields and config snapshot fields) with compatibility shims or mechanical refactors to avoid partial convention islands.

findings.summary: Several public names do not describe behavior, especially around Wi-Fi and service startup.
evidence: `ConstructWiFiInfo(dev string) error` performs state refresh side effects and returns no constructed value (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go:86-102`).

`Start*WithContext` naming is overloaded: some functions supervise worker errors (`StartVersionSubsystemWithContext`, `StartDockerSubsystem`), while others only spawn goroutines and wait for cancellation (`StartBackupRoutinesWithContext`, `StartChopRoutinesWithContext`).
This mismatch forces callers to inspect implementations to know whether `Start` means 'initialize and return', 'run until cancel', or 'spawn background and block on context'.
findings.suggestion: Rename APIs to behavior-oriented verbs (`RefreshWiFiInfo`, `Run...Loop`, `Supervise...`) and reserve `Start*` for a single semantic. Update startup wiring to rely on the renamed categories so call-site intent is explicit.
